content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Any recent films that felt like incredible, unique, new movie experiences?

**Score:** 68
**Body:** I recently listened to a podcast that discussed movies it would be great to be able to experience again for the first time. They had a good list and I thought of a few others. The Matrix, Lord of the Rings, Jurassic Park, Star Wars, The Sixth Sense, Inception, Memento were some that came up. 

Have you seen any more modern movies that you feel had some incredible, unique, "game changing" sort of impact for you like these movies?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Triangle of sadness
2. The Green Knight

Mandy

Eddington 

Melancholia

The Florida Project 
  - Mandy for the win!
3. "Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die" (2025)  

This one is a real melon-popper.
  - Check Lola 2022, Palm Springs 2020, One Cut of the Dead 2017, The Similars 2015, Dream Scenario 2023, Ink 2009, The Frame 2014, They cloned Tyrone, Colossal 2016 and The Incident 2014. If you haven’t that is. They might be of interest to you considering you mention that one. 
4. I would say Midsommar, I find it to be incredibly unique and I still find myself thinking about it occasionally and referencing it in conversations.
5. For me, Dune. I feel like I haven’t seen a movie respect its source material while making smart on screen changes to the story like that since LOTR. While there have been many genre movies between LOTR and Dune, I feel like Dune nails the spirit of the source material while feeling unlike any blockbuster I have ever seen. It ends up feeling epic in a way that little else does. I’m a Villeneuve Stan though. 
  - Villeneuve is a movie making genius. How he managed to make Arrival both a decent cinema popcorn flick and deeply, intelligently layered without it being Nolan-esque pretentious is impressive. 
6. Poor Things 
7. Hundreds of Beavers. 

I don't know that it will influence movies going forward, but it was definitely unique, enjoyable, and I had never seen anything like it. 

## 2. Looking for movies where the “victim” turns out to be the real nightmare

**Score:** 49
**Body:** Hey everyone,

I’m looking for movies with a very specific vibe. The setup is something like a kidnapping, home invasion, or random crime where you *think* the main character is the victim… but then it flips. Instead of just defending themselves, they turn into something way more dangerous and start actively hunting the bad guys.

Basically, the movie shifts into a survival story — but for the villains.

Not really looking for pure revenge-after-the-fact stories. I want that moment where the criminals realize they picked the *wrong* person and now they’re trapped in a nightmare.

Bonus points if it has:

* Psychological tension / mind games
* Cat-and-mouse hunting vibes
* A big tone shift halfway through
* A creepy or unpredictable “victim”

Any recommendations? Even lesser-known or foreign films are welcome.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1.  Hard Candy
2. Home Alone 
  - This made me actually laugh out loud because it hits the nail right on the head, swung from a paint tin on a rope.
3. Abigail (2024) for sure. 🤌
4. Primal Fear with Edward Norton and Richard Gere. 
5. Gone Girl.   
Rambo, Last Blood. 

6. You're Next 
7. Strange Darling
8. High Tension
9. Secret Window (2004) an underrated Johnny Depp role.

## 3. Movies like Project Hail Mary

**Score:** 38
**Body:** It's a new movie so I won't any spoilers... but just in the mood for long sci-fi movies that take place off-planet Earth.

The movie was really engrossing and epic-length. Don't think have seen a movie that's near 3 hours in a long time. Anything along the same kind of vibe as Project Hail Mary? 



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I will add Moon (2009) with Sam Rockwell 
2. * *Pandorum* (2009)
* *Passengers* (2016)
* *Sunshine* (2007)
  - That’s a solid 3 I love Danny boyles sunshine!
He also made the “28 days” series and Trainspotting
3. The Martian (from a novel with the same author as Project Hail Mary)
4. Aniara is a great movie. Also check out the animated series Scavengers Reign. Best Sci-Fi I’ve seen in a long time.
5. Sunshine (2007) mission to save earth from freezing, also traveling towards a dying sun, tonally different
6. Couldn't possibly be more unlike Project Hail Mary, but Solaris (1972) by Andrei Tarkovsky is tremendously long and takes place in space.
7. Interstellar/Moon are great, but they’re not really the same vibe. PHM works because it’s smart + funny + emotional all at once.

The Martian is the obvious pick, but after that I’d go:

•	⁠Into the Spider-Verse
•	⁠The LEGO Movie
•	⁠Guardians of the Galaxy
•	⁠Edge of Tomorrow

Different genres, same “this is fun as hell but also hits you in the feels” energy.
8. Prometheus 




9. Interstellar

## 4. movies about autistic adults?

**Score:** 24
**Body:** i am autistic and i find it very difficult to find movies that portray the actual autistic experience without characters being portrayed as stereotypical (geniuses, creeps, idk really anything). I would really appreciate suggestions for movies that feature autistic adults, not just movies where the main character is a little bit quirky. Of course i do not mind if it is a headcanon/non-canon that the character is autistic, but i would prefer movies that focus on that characters life rather than a super action-heavy plot, though im not opposed to it as long as the characters seem real and living. I know this is kind of specific and if it doesn’t make sense i can clarify I just don’t really know a better way to explain it! 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There's an autistic character in The Pitt and the show portrays how autism can cause people to be overwhelmed and feel things very strongly, rather than portraying autism as a genius superpower. 
  - And highlights her caretaker relationship with her autistic sister. Dr King is a great character, and Taylor Dearden who played her is neurodivergent herself (and Bryan Cranston’s daughter). She’s one of my favorite characters on the show. 
2. Temple Grandin (2010)
  - I haven't seen it, but just want to say that someone told me they saw it and said a whole bunch of stuff to me using that movie, basically comparing my level 1 autism to temple grandin's autism. so, just putting it out there that it MAY not be the best movie for representation. especially since it didnt do a good job of educating this person who spoke to me about it
3. Please stand by 
4. Legally Blonde

I'm not saying they intended to write Elle Woods as an autistic woman who flies under the radar due to her stereotypically feminine interests, but... 
  - I love this take
5. Honestly i absolutely love Mitchell vs machines and the son is on the spectrum for sure and imo the dad is very very likely to be too, love that movie


6. Bugonia (2025) has an autistic character portrayed by an autistic actor (Aidan Delbis) and he is fantastic.

[He did a great interview about it](https://www.latimes.com/entertainment-arts/movies/story/2025-10-22/aidan-delbis-was-still-in-high-school-when-he-landed-a-lead-role-in-bugonia) a few months ago.
  - ooooh okay okay bugonia was definitely a movie i had been planning to watch so this has bumped it up the list!

## 5. Movies to watch with my 16-year old sister

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Hello. I (17M) and my sister (16) want to watch movies for the entire day today since our parents are out of town. We've watched a lot of films in our life, so i'd say we're pretty much knowledgeable in that field. 

Any genre is fine, except horror. And films shouldn't have very much sex/nudity in them. 

Thanks in advance!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Big Fish, Second Hand Lions,  Monty Walsh, Jessie Stone series,  The End, Airplane, Breakfast Club.
2. Here are some random mostly 90s movies I love that you might not have seen:

My Cousin Vinny

The Birdcage

Willow

Empire Records

Harold and Maude

A League of Their Own

Captain Ron

The Last of the Mohicans

The Crow

Tank Girl

Tombstone

Demolition Man
3. Guessing you've seen these, but if not: Lord of the Rings trilogy, Indianma Jones movies, any of the YA films like Hunger Games, Maze Runner, etc. Pirates of the Caribbean series.
4. Little Miss Sunshine
5. *The Princess Bride, Stardust, The Adventures of Baron Munchausen,* and *Hook* all go together very nicely

If you haven't had the pleasure yet, 20 years ago Stephen Chow gave us the incredible 1-2 slam dunk combo of *Shaolin Soccer* and *Kung Fu Hustle*, two of the greatest martial arts action comedies of all time.
6.  Winter, Spring, Summer or Fall, The Edge of Seventeen and Chemical Hearts (Indy Coming of Age sort type of films)
7. korean:  Dude In Me, Luck Key, Dream, Little Forest, Golden Holiday, Mrs. and Miss Cop, Castaway on the Moon, Going By the Book
8. City of Lost Children.  French, but subtitles make you pay attention 
9. Maybe some classics if you guys havent caught them? Ferris Bueller, The Princess Bride or a bit more modern with Ever After and Ten Things I Hate About You. 
You could do some sets like The Matrix or Men in Black or Toy Story. 
 
10. The Peanut Butter Falcon

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Till this day, the ending of the Mist movie still messed me up

**Score:** 2383
**Body:** It’s unanimous that the Mist ending is so dark and messed up. I understand that David did not want his son and the adults to get killed by the monster but he should’ve waited a little longer.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This guy has the worst luck, Family  ran over, had to go to the center of the earth, has to deal with mutated sharks. Stay safe Thomas Jane!
  - Dude even died crash landing into Venus 
    - Doors and corners ....
2. Funny enough in the movie the religious nutter said if they sacrifice the child it would end and sure enough in the ending when he shoots the kid it does end.
  - ![gif](giphy|1hMk0bfsSrG32Nhd5K)


  - Woah, I never put the two together. This has to be intentional! I gotta rewatch this movie now. 
    - Plus, there’s that crazy bit where on a truck full of people that passes David, the woman who walked out of the market earlier to get to her kids survived. Why and how, we never know.
      - The Mist is a metaphor for losing hope/humanity, and she immediately did the most human, hopeful thing and sacrificed her own safety because her kids needed her. Everyone else started prioritizing their own survival. When the Mist blankets the town, that is the moment people begin losing their humanity (as evidenced by the fact that nobody offers to help her)
3. One of the few times changing the ending from the source material was to the better. 
  - Even stephen king said he wished he had thought of this ending when the master of horror says your version is darker than his you know you won

## 2. What is the greatest ending scene you have ever seen in a movie? My pick is the ending of The Usual Suspects.

**Score:** 502

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shawshank Redemption - Red walking on the beach with Andy fixing a boat.
2. https://preview.redd.it/fbg26w8hy5xg1.jpeg?width=1650&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=e8aec53f55edb7e3f7e43bdbc04a275d7cbefe4b

Primal Fear. Masterclass bait and switch!
  - I just love how at the very end he’s saying stuff like “ahhh cmon man… it’s like we were dancing, Marty” 🤣….while Richard Gere just looks so disappointed in himself for being fooled.  Absolutely hilarious the way Ed Norton just makes him feel so stupid there at the end after thinking he’s so in control and being so sure of himself the whole movie up until that point.  
3. Seven
  - Spacey is strong in this thread
    - DeeeeteeeeeCTIVE!!!
    - American Beauty, not the “greatest” ending, but was memorable
4. Apocalypto 
5. *Raiders Of The Lost Ark.*


Both the climax, and the twist.


Top. Men.
6. Larry.. I'm sorry.. I'm a cop

![gif](giphy|9qr1BlOJGldyU)

## 3. Love the movie 13th Warrior, but as an Arab I can’t lie, seeing Ibn Fadlan clean-shaven always felt a bit off to me. So I edited in a beard to see how it might’ve looked.

**Score:** 347

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Although historically inaccurate, I still prefer clean shaven Banderas in the movie. It adds to the contrast between unruly and uncivilized Vikings (with their beards and all) with civilized (clean shaven) Arab narrative / visual esthetic.

Look, this film rules ok? It has absolutely zero things that needs changing.
  - I prefer to think the Vikings were initially pretending to be slobs in front of him as a joke. Vikings were actually quite fastidious with grooming. It is a solid Beowulf movie.
  - Literally the only change I would make to this movie is the miraculous reappearance of McTiernan’s version of it, because there was apparently a lot more of the movie in it.
2. And Omar Sharif just sitting there, “am I a joke to you?”
3. Ibn! I thought this movie fuckin ripped when I was a kid. Also introduced me to Michael Crichton and Beowulf.
4. Lo, there do I see my father.
Lo, there do I see my mother, and my sisters, and my brothers.
Lo, there do I see the line of my people, back to the beginning.
Lo, they do call to me.
They bid me take my place among them, in the halls of Valhalla!
Where the brave may live forever."
Remember watching this movie with my dad as a kid and loved it.
5. He wasn't technically an Arab. Wasn't he from Al Andalus, Islamic occupied Spain? 
  - He was born in Baghdad.
6. No offence to Antonio Banderas but watching a Spaniard play an Arab always felt a bit off to me.
  - Better than a Scotsman playing a Spaniard! 

## 4. What do you think? Who do you think are the top 3 that are most talented in this list? Mine would be the Skarsgard bros and Margaret Qualley!

**Score:** 338

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jared Harris deserves all the accolades 
  - TIL Jared Harris is a nepo baby. That’s crazy!
2. The Skarsgards remain in my top two, then it roared depending on the role. 
  - When your dad has 200% of a top tier actor’s talent, then it’s not nepotism. It’s biology. 
  - Skarsgards are funny because Alexander has been established for a *while*, and a lot of people who were watching True Blood (which was an enormous show) when they were like 15 genuinely would have known him before and better than Stellan. To make us all feel old, they’d be in their late 30s now fyi. 
    - He’ll always be Meekus in Zoolander to me. 
      - Orange Mocha Frappuccino!
3. Domhnall Gleeson is a glaring omission here. Great actor
4. I bet there's a lot of actors this person likes that he doesn't even realize are nepo babies
5. Ray Nicholson is an EXCELLENT actor. He has his father’s chaotic energy. 

He is absolutely wonderful in Borderline. 

## 5. Charlize Theron in Monster delivers one of the most transformative, terrifying performances ever

**Score:** 306
**Body:** Rewatched this film recently after so many years and what Charlize does here is nothing short of amazing. 

Her performance as Aileen Wuornos is flawless. 

She completely throws herself into the role and disappears. It’s not just the makeup but her expressions and delivery that are just spot on.

One of the most well deserved oscar wins and definitely up there with one of her best performances. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. She did a really good job
  - She disappears completely in the role. This is a prime example of a transformative performance. 
    - When she breaks down crying after discovering the betrayal was her girlfriend all along, I couldn't help but cry too. I saw the documentary made with the real Warren with my mom last year and it was pretty damn heartbreaking in a morbid kind of way. I was shocked at the amount of time the justice system seemed to drag her sentence on and on with more trials even when she had been constantly appealing to simply get on with it and die. The victims, I think, in a way, included Aileen herself at some points along the way. The system failed everyone involved. 
  - Well that's why she won an Oscar. 
2. her range is incredible. Icon frankly 
  - She’s one of the best actresses working today. 

Loved her in Mad Max Fury Road. She deserved a nomination there 
    - no argument here 
3. Yeah, “transformative” is the perfect word. She was unrecognizable compared to her usual dolled-up starlet image, and the performance itself was just as raw and convincing.
4. Great depiction. Quite possibly the best depiction of a serial killer ever. 

Sidebar: If you’re interested in the subject of Aileen Wuornos and how the entire world failed her up and to her execution watch the Nick Broomfields docs “the selling of a serial killer” and “Aileen: life and death of a serial killer”. I’m not one to create narratives to create sympathetic characters out of murderers but from her early childhood up to her execution she was used, abused, and abandoned by everyone around her. It’s a look into how cruel the world can be. 
  - Wholeheartedly agree.

Her whole backstory is quite insightful.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What forgotten movies do you think will be reclaimed by future cineastes?

**Score:** 89
**Body:** Obviously, there’s a wealth of great films that were hated or forgotten upon release, but later gained a greater following and are now regarded favorably by the cinephile world. Recently, the critical reappraisal of the Wachowski’s *Speed Racer* got me thinking about this, but there are countless other examples (*Showgirls*, *Fire: Walk With Me*, *The Thing*)

Many of these feature a lot of common traits: 

1. Almost all of them are made by auteur filmmakers.

2. They feature taboo or transgressive content, or otherwise use familiar concepts or material in a subversive way.

3. Their initial critical failure is due to intentional stylistic choices, rather than a lack of command over the form. In other words, you get the sense that everything unconventional about the film was done on purpose. 

4. Formal experimentation, the use of cinematic language in a way that directly engages with (or alienates) the audience. 

This makes me wonder, what movies will cinephiles of the next generation rediscover? My money is on CGI-era Tim Burton. Or perhaps M. Night Shyamalan’s “bad” movies like *The Happening* and *The Village*. What does everyone here think? 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Strange Days (1995) is a criminally underrated gem that is stacked with talent and sadly has become increasingly relevant in the 2020s vs the 90s

Starring prime Ralph Fiennes and Angela Bassett fresh off of Oscar nominations

Directed by Kathryn Bigelow, screenplay by James Cameron

Some truly innovative camera work (they basically had to invent GoPro cameras to make the movie)

And the science fiction elements feel increasingly real in today’s social media obsessed brainrotted cultural landscape
  - Strange Days has definitely had a reappraisal. Letterboxed put out a [good article](https://letterboxd.com/journal/strange-days-kathryn-bigelow-anniversary-retrospective/) on it last year. I really enjoy the movie despite also having a few issues with it. But sometimes a flawed but ambitious movie can be just as enjoyable (or more so) than a perfect film 
    - The >!'few bad apples' ending and the good commissioner coming in to save the day!< left such a sour taste in my mouth, it didn't seem to align with the point you were led to think the movie was making at all up until then. Kind of diminished the whole thing for me.
      - That's my biggest issue with the film as well. Perhaps it would've been too edgy to have the film ending in widespread race riots, but when that is the plot it decided to go with, it's strange to press right up against the line of radical racial justice >!before retreating into the safety of a trusted authority figure. "Don't worry!" Strange Days says, "there will always be a good cop to keep the bad cops in line. It's just a few bad apples causing all this ruckus." How convenient that the police department never has to face public scrutiny. I say release the tape! Justice for Jeriko!!<
2. Well, neither of those examples had ever been “forgotten”; they just underwent a re-evaluation. Also, I’m not quite sure *Showgirls* was ever evaluated above the status of high camp. Examples that fit the label of reclaimed forgotten films might be *Peeping Tom* or *The Night of the Hunter*. I’m really having a hard time thinking about contemporary equivalences, since so many films just slip under the radar these days thanks to streaming. A film that I was thinking about lately was *Under the Silver Lake*, since not only was it critically trashed, but also dumped out of cinemas quickly and almost never talked about.
  - Under the Silver Lake is a great shout. I watched it when it first came out and remember being flabbergasted that no one was talking about it and that it had been shrugged off by critics. 
  - The sub for Under the Silver Lake is one of the most entertaining rabbit holes you can go down on this site. Highly recommend if you're even slightly into schizo leaning real-world conspiracy/mystery stuff
3.  I don't know if it will happen, but I would love to see people take a closer look at Japanese films from the the the time before and after WWII. It feels like all that every gets discussed are 2 Ozu Films, a handful of samurai films, and the complete works of Akira Kurosawa. It's always so strange seeing people come and post on places like this talking about how they've just watched every Kurosawa film but then that will be the sum total of their exposure to Japanese films from that era. Directors such as Naruse and Kinoshita seem to be totally unknown to western film fans. I remember when Juliette Binoche and Ralph Fiennes went to the criterion closest they randomly pulled out the collection of silent Naruse films and neither of them had ever heard of him.
4. I think *Prometheus* fell victim to a kind of, in my eyes nitpicky hate campaign by the more superficial nerd types who overfocused on these kinds of imho not so relevant plotholes, while ignoring the impressive philosophical grandiosity of the movie.


I can totally see and admit what they mean, it really had a couple of strong lapses in that plothoie vein, but I think that discussion totally over shadowed the immense strengths of the movie.


I think the central themes around self-creation and self-sacrifice are and will be highly relevant to actual political discourse around climate change and dwindling ressources, and the movie expressed them very poignantly and elegantly.


I would hope people will reasses the movie in appreciation of those terms and a bit more gracefully overlook those few, admitted, lapses it has.
  - In addition to your point, the production design is just really impressive in line with the work done on the first two Alien movies

## 2. Is there anything Under the Silver Lake (2018)? What did you make of it?

**Score:** 33
**Body:** I enjoyed Under the Silver Lake. It was a bit like if Robert Altman’s The Long Goodbye had been remade by David Lynch, with a touch of The Big Lebowski. However while it was enjoyable on a scene-by-scene basis, I never really understood what it was all about, apart from a vague “something about conspiracies”. Did it all add up to anything in the end? Or was it just a big shaggy dog story? And is that a problem, as long as it’s enjoyable? What, if anything, do you think the director was trying to say?  

To me it also raised some questions about cultural specificity. As I’m from the UK and only know LA from films, I didn’t know going in that the Silver Lake is a real place. It looks in the film like it’s not a super-rich area, but presumably it’s near other areas that are much richer. It’s not clear from the film itself, and I don’t know if it’s relevant. 

Are there any other cultural touchstones in the film that are obvious to people from LA, but go over the heads of everyone else? Are there really a collection of urban myths associated with the Silver Lake area, for example? Not knowing these things didn’t affect my enjoyment, but perhaps they would help to make sense of what happens in the film. 

It strikes me that this is probably quite a polarizing film, so I thought it would be interesting to ask what everyone else’s experiences with it are, and to ask if anyone can shed any light on what's going on. 



### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Quite a few internet-sleuths have delved deep into all the codes and hidden messages in the film, not the surface level narrative ones but all matter of little easter eggs. All things considered they exist more apart from the film, so probably won't tell or give you anything new with regards to your specific points, but if the film intrigues you then it's at the very least an interesting YouTube rabbit hole to dive into for a while. 

To your points, I do think that like Lynch's work, the film is ultimately more of a feeler than a thinker, and I think you got the gist of its message just fine. It is both a love letter to Hollywood, L.A., and American popular culture as a whole, as well as a condemnation of that industry's awful, capitalist, abusive, exploitative, misogynist, seedy underbelly.
  - Great points, thanks!
2. there are a lot of cool references and definitely a lot of suggestive depth, but i would restrain yourself from seeking out a resolving answer. the movie itself seems to be exploring this very mechanism of desiring after a resolution that can never arrive. 

its a bit ambiguous (ok a lot) about a sort of schizophrenic affect, and how much schizophrenia (delusional insular thinking, beliefs of grandiosity, irrational behavior) is our self vs our environment. we really do live in a society overwhelmed with coded messages, people really are engaged in conspiracies all around us, and the film doesn't really give us a way to live with this except as a sort of apathetic resignation or delusional mania.

examining the inciting incident of a hot woman showing and then withdrawing interest, this is a fundamental male anxiety and one where the truth is too uncomfortable to face, so he redresses it as part of the grand conspiracy behind it all. it's the Blue Velvet problem and solution: reality conforms to our needs, and what Sam seeks after is a reality that needs him rather than discards him. enter delusion.

and the film is very careful to not paint Sam out as a mastermind either, the fact that he is endlessly distracted by women is another pointer. the conspiracy might be the ostensible destination, but the road is filled with women to ogle and chase after. that delusion can reframe base desires is shown with the other conspiracist as well who muses "I should have a family, who else would I leave this to" again he feels filled with transcendent meaning and a desire to transmit it and the result is a very familiar base desire, and one that the conspiracy/strangeness is really actively impeding.

one canon the film is steeped in that warrants mention is the collapse of media as separate from the consciousness that consumes it, or put another way the image has become a medium of consciousness, the dissolution of witness and participant. Blow Up, Blow Out, Videodrome, the Conversation, all of these movies are fascinated by this idea, and i think that's actually the clearer lineage than Lynch (though i do think Lynch looms over the film, he's just not alone)

im not really an authority to speak on any of the LA specific stuff (outside of some film reference ive picked up) but i dont think any of it ends up being important, but once again doing a similar sort of 'oh im decoding this grand mystery but it requires me developing a lot of trendy hipster ingroup knowledge' in the way that it also requires interacting with lots of hot women.
3. >However while it was enjoyable on a scene-by-scene basis, I never really understood what it was all about, 

The California detective movie is a staple in American cinema: a man living in California who is financially/socially down and out/down on his luck because he is too smart for normal work and too alienated for smart people work meets a beautiful woman in distress, is drawn into a conspiracy of rich white men while investigating her murder, and is either killed by or exposes the conspiracy.

Under the Silver Lake is a pastiche of this genre.

>Or was it just a big shaggy dog story? 

Not really.

Garfield's character discovers that a business is plying a conspiracy to convince wealthy men to seal themselves into tombs with 3 prostitutes to "live forever."

Along the way he encounters multiple people who are living out their own horror movies which are only tangentially connected with his story.
 
>And is that a problem, as long as it’s enjoyable? What, if anything, do you think the director was trying to say? 

People are primed to conspiratorial thinking. And men, generally, suck.

>It looks in the film like it’s not a super-rich area, but

The average house price in Silver Lake is ~$1.5 million.

>Are there really a collection of urban myths associated with the Silver Lake area, for example?

Every community in America has a collection of urban myths.
4. Personally I found the ideas it was talking about were a little obvious to me. It seemed to me about a guy who wanted to see conspiracy in a lot of things and was obsessed with something out there being the problem in his life, but not realising that the real conspiracy was just good old mundane rich people and capitalism and all the other stuff just obscures that and fights ghosts when he could be fighting something important.
5. Don’t have much to contribute to your questions or analyses but just wanted to say this film has been on my mind recently. 

With all the news of elite sex-rings, ritualistic abuse of women, coded messages and symbology, prophecies of the end times it strikes me as very prescient & typifies the current confusing times we live in. 

Will give it another rewatch soon.  
6. Watched this recently - the general reaction to it on reddit and elsewhere confuses me. It felt like a fairly straightforward movie, at least thematically, having several points where characters essentially just look into the camera and tell you what it's about (e.g. with the Songwriter, the rich guy in the shack, the Homeless King, Andrew Garfield's character ranting about the homeless).

I think Under the Silver Lake's main theme is "modern patriarchy" - women being valued more pornographically rather than for their reproductive labour, extreme capitalist wealth inequality, mass culture, yadda yadda yadda - and the way this suffuses culture top to bottom, from the structure of Jesus & the Brides of Dracula mirroring the cultic harem, Andrew Garfield's character and the Songwriter really just wanting their own cultic harems but being unable to get them, the women in the harems not being able to envision a future for themselves besides prostituting themselves in the peak of their youth and then dying, then rich men not wanting to do anything with their wealth besides gooning with their harem until they die, Andrew Garfield's character accepting a mirror of the harem arrangement in the end by hooking up with the older lady for a place to stay, the ubiquitous sexualized ads and Andrew Garfield's character literally cumming to revelation looking at them, and so on. And as u/unrealisedpotential notes, Weinstein, Epstein, etc. Themes and such.
7. Like any "cult" movie, theories abound and range from the obvious to the insightful to the unhinged. For me it's an autopsy on the ontology of LA, an exploration of apophenia, and a post-modern genre riff with a rich lineage that starts with Chandler, and more specifically The Big Sleep, as a means to make a psychogeographical map of the City of Angels.


I could tell you what I think but where's the fun in that? Better to find your own route (from the back of cereal boxes maybe?) and reach your own destination.


Here's a brief timeline of touchstones to get you started: 


The Big Sleep, Hitchcock (Rear Window and Vertigo especially), In a Lonely Place, Kiss Me Deadly, Kenneth Anger, Harper, Putney Swope, what happened at Lookout Mountain?, Vito Paulekas and his freaks, The Crying of Lot 49,  The Long Goodbye, Chinatown, Three Women, Day of the Locust, Phantom of the Paradise, the history of MCA and Lew Wasserman, Body Double, Repo Man, Ellroy's LA Quartet, The Big Lebowski,  City of Quartz by Mike Davis, the documentary Los Angeles Plays Itself, Adam Curtis's The Trap, Inherent Vice (book and film), Atlanta's Teddy episode...

Happy hunting.
8. I've always interpreted this film as a depiction of a young man going through the onset of schizophrenia or something similar. 


The world has its own internal logic to it that doesn't really make sense to anyone outside of it.  There are a few scenes that feel like how a person with schizophrenia would perceive something banal like getting picked up by police for loitering or similar and turn it into an adventure with someone going by 'the Homeless King.'


This is, of course, my purely personal belief.  But I've had conversations with people going through mental health episodes that feel like they were recounting the plot of this movie.
  - That’s a very interesting interpretation. I did wonder if there’s any significance to the fact that the protagonist doesn’t have a job and doesn’t seem to be trying to get one. He’s quite frazzled and weird, but he’s not depicted as a loser exactly, and he clearly has no problem attracting the ladies. This could all just be movie logic though, where female characters are often attracted to guys like that. It’s hard to say what the “rules” of this film are.

## 3. Mother Mary and the Out of Control Allegory

**Score:** 16
**Body:** David Lowery's Mother Mary is close to being something of a parody of A24 "elevated horror." I still enjoyed it, but depicting the relatively more mundane with such a weighty allegorical language feels... overdone?

The Christian symbolism is so pervasive that it's almost comical how seemingly *everything* is somehow a biblical reference, and really, I don't see the point in that level of excess or what this adds other than dressing the story up in something more grand than it really is.

Sam's religious upbringing very clearly influences her work, the way she acts, the way she speaks- and her molding Mary (who gives up her agency early on) into this Marian pop idol is an expression of that. At this point it seems that all of this Biblical imagery is allegorical, but then near the end of the movie- Sam's assistant (an angel?) is dictating to Sam (God the Father) what Mary (Mary) is doing- meaning that she was a more literal version of what seemed to be a metaphor (which also comes up earlier).

Because *s*o many aspects of the film seem to have allegorical ties it became too much to keep track of which of these were relevant and which were superficial, and although where allegory starts and stops is a theme that's brought up in the movie- this was not relevant until the end.

What did this have to say about Christianity? I would say it's a positive depiction of Mary and her relationship to God and the world as well as a critique of a perceived modern hyper-Marianism, with that perceived heresy being mended as the resolution. I emphasize *perceived* since the idea that Mary is being worshipped by Catholics/Orthodox Christians has been a consistent Protestant misconception, and a decent portion of the film is dedicated to talking about how Mary left Sam and that the door was closed etc. I am not particularly versed on Catholic church history so maybe I missed some of the text of the movie.

I am definitely left wondering where allegory begins and ends, and the non-stop biblical references I think obfuscate more than elucidate a message, but I really want to hear what others think about this and hopefully someone can tell me how I'm wrong because I would really like to like this movie more!

\*edit  
Some more thoughts after reading a few interviews, watching the [IndieWire interview](https://youtu.be/7c95ixuVR74?si=MffzLU4_c22P1oLa).  
Abusing iconography to superficially deepen a film is how I think I would try to succinctly describe what I feel is going on here. Everything is 'labeled' and those 'labels' are so grand and lofty while being stuck onto... not necessarily the mundane-- but definitely something markedly *different.*  
Are Mary and Sam's relationship sort of like Mary and God? Maybe...? But the film does not show you how that could be- it just puts that label on them and lets the implied allegory thread them together lazily.

I felt a similar way with the look of Green Knight, where Lowery wants to use such heady iconography but doesn't have anything grand enough to apply it to. So now we have two aesthetically rich films with narratives that can't match that.
    - ugh Sam was SUCH a thinly-conceived character. you're so right about the uneven tone – the flowery monologues were so ill-fitting for two late-30s women who have been friends since they were teens! 

it's even more glaring because Michaela is playing the exact same archetype in another film in theaters right now: *The Christophers*. she probably speaks half as much dialogue in that one, but her character is more fully-realized, and her relationship with her co-star follows a much more intelligible arc. 

Soderbergh has spoken about realizing that his writing was holding back the quality of film he could pull off, and has relationships with many great writers (Ed Solomon on this latest one). Lowery is such an incredible director and I wish he would do the same!

## 4. Certain Women (2016) disturbing content warning?

**Score:** 9
**Body:** I just watched Certain Women on Kanopy, a great film by Kelly Reichardt. I’ve watched a few movies on there but it’s the first one that had a “disturbing content” warning at the beginning. Unfortunately that warning had me bracing the whole movie, for… what? What was the disturbing content?

My review to hit the character count: I loved this movie. I thought the first act was brilliant. Then the second act had me scratching my head. Then, the third act was SO good, it almost made the first act look bad (a bit heavy-handed), and made the second act look better.

## 5. Kiki's Delivery Service Review

**Score:** 2
**Body:** Today I watched Kiki's Delivery Service. Kiki is a witch who, like every other witch, has to move to a new place to make a new start. It’s a coming of age movie where we see Kiki struggling and dealing with confidence issues.

​The movie was character driven, which is why we don't see any action-packed fighting in the climax, instead, we see Kiki regaining her confidence, which makes her powers start working again. The animation was very good for its time, and the music was quite nice too. I also liked the world building of the movie.

​While watching, I was waiting for a villain reveal like in Disney movies, but that didn't happen. It’s not that I’m disappointed, I just found it to be a really good coming of age film. Overall, it was a great experience as a comfort watch, and I’d probably want to watch it again with my younger siblings.
